title: Algebraic Tomography of Non-Hermitian Floquet Systems from Observable Traces
abstract: We formulate a framework of Floquet algebraic tomography for finite-dimensional non-Hermitian monodromy matrices from observable trace sequences $ζ_n^{(O)}={\rm Tr}(OM^n)$. Since these sequences are constrained by the characteristic polynomial of $M$, the inverse problem is a finite-dimensional algebraic reconstruction problem rather than a generic exponential fit. We organize the reconstruction through the observable resolvent, spectral determinant, and Dirichlet spectral data, separating the common spectral skeleton from observable-dependent dressing. Cayley--Hamilton and Hankel methods recover the similarity-invariant spectral data, while multi-observable and Liouville-space extensions connect the construction to realization theory and tomography reconstruction. We further clarify the limits of identifiability from restricted observable algebras: the data determine a visible representative, micromotion can enlarge the sampled visible operator space, and exact symmetries impose residual invisible sectors. Two examples, a driven transmon qutrit and a finite non-Hermitian Floquet SSH chain, demonstrate leakage-induced visibility expansion, observable-dependent phase response, EP-accessible branch geometry, and disorder/probe-dependent observable-dimension readouts.
summary: This paper introduces a method called Floquet algebraic tomography to reconstruct the underlying dynamics of non-Hermitian periodic systems from measurement traces. It distinguishes between the system's intrinsic spectrum and the effects of the chosen measurement probe, providing a way to identify spectral features like Exceptional Points even when parts of the system are hidden by symmetry.
main_problem: Developing an algebraic framework to reconstruct the spectral properties and monodromy matrix of non-Hermitian Floquet systems from limited, time-discrete observable trace data.
main_result: The authors establish a 'Floquet algebraic tomography' framework that separates intrinsic spectral features from observable-dependent dressing, proving that while certain parts of the dynamics may remain invisible due to symmetries, the 'visible' part of the monodromy matrix can be reconstructed using Cayley-Hamilton and Hankel methods.
method: The framework utilizes Observable Trace Sequences (OTS), the Observable Resolvent, and Cayley-Hamilton recurrences, alongside Hankel matrix constructions and Prony-type methods for spectral reconstruction.
model_or_system: Finite-dimensional non-Hermitian Floquet systems characterized by a monodromy matrix M in GL(N, C), with specific applications to a driven transmon qutrit and a finite non-Hermitian Floquet SSH chain.
key_observables: Observable Trace Sequence (OTS) defined as Tr(OM^n), observable resolvent, spectral determinant, and Dirichlet spectral data.
important_parameters: Spectral regimes including Simple Spectra (SS), Diabolic Points (DP), and Exceptional Points (EP); observable dimension and algebraic deficiency.
main_assumptions: The system is finite-dimensional, the monodromy matrix is assumed to be stationary/time-periodic, and the observable O is independent of the discrete period index n.
figures_summary: Figure 9 demonstrates the growth of observable dimension as a function of micromotion slices, comparing clean, bond-disordered, and on-site-disordered configurations in an SSH chain.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the inverse problem of reconstruction, develops the algebraic framework (resolvent, determinant, and Dirichlet data), provides the reconstruction engine (Cayley-Hamilton and Hankel methods), addresses identifiability and symmetry-induced invisibility, and validates the theory with transmon and SSH chain examples.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous algebraic toolset for characterizing the dynamics of open, driven quantum systems (like transmons or topological chains) where only partial, stroboscopic measurements are available, specifically addressing the complex physics of Exceptional Points.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Reconstruction errors for the DTQ3 from a single observable Oqub. The errors in the reconstructed eigenvalues and characteristic coefficients remain close to machine precision over the full scan, showing that a single observable channel already recovers the full GL(3, C) spectral skeleton.

FIG. 2: Near-degeneracy point characterized in the two-parameter plane of the bare energy E1 and the drive amplitude A. (Left) Color plot of the minimal spectral gap δλ on a log10 scale. (Right) The E1-dependence of the spectral gap δλ and the matrix condition number κ(U) evaluated at a fixed drive amplitude A = 0.85, demonstrating the stable, DP nature of the degeneracy.

FIG. 3: Complex-plane trajectories of the ODSD F(O)(p) for p = 0 and p = 1 under the variation of the bare energy E1 ∈[0.02, 2.02]. We compare the total qubit population Oqub with the population difference Odiff. The continuous deformation of these loops under drive-induced dressing highlights the onset of severe destructive interference, serving as the geometric precursor to the quantized visibility jumps shown in Fig. 4.

FIG. 4: (Left) Drive-induced redistribution of the mode-wise phase accumulation observed by Oqub as a function of the drive amplitude A. (Right) The visibility loss ∆(O) vis (p) for p = 0, 1 evaluated for the total population Oqub and the difference observable Odiff. The sharp, quantized steps in the visibility loss reflect winding-changing events where the continuous deformation of the dressing coefficients forces the complex trajectory of F(O)(p) to cross the origin, abruptly shedding a full 2π phase rotation.

FIG. 5: Sampled observable dimension Dobs = rank(K0) in the DTQ3 model plotted against the number of micromotion slices Q. In the isolated qubit limit (B = 0), the observable remains trapped within a one-dimensional invariant sector (Dobs = 1). In contrast, turning on coherent leakage (B = 0.47) dynamically accesses the third level, triggering an algebraic expansion of the visible operator space that reaches the full qutrit operator-space dimension N 2 = 9.
